metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix the A/B harness baseline so it is genuinely discriminating on the fixture's attr space — either define the gate against a minimum absolute separation floor (e.g. `sep_new ≥ 0.40` and `median_NIT < 0.30`) rather than `sep_new > sep_baseline ≈ 0`, or populate the fixture with both old and new attrs so the kernel mean can actually score it; document which comparison the plan intends.
findings.finding: The A/B harness baseline (kernel mean `impact`) cannot meaningfully score the labeled fixture because the fixture attrs contain only the new consequence binaries (e.g. `data_loss_without_recovery`, `implicit_coupling`, `churn90`) — not the old mean-model attrs (`prod_impact`, `debt_impact`, `blast_radius`, `likelihood`, `reversibility`). Calling `impact(attrs)` on any fixture row returns ~0.25 for both HIGH and NIT, making baseline separation structurally ~0. The `sep_new > sep_baseline` exit-nonzero gate therefore reduces to `sep_new > ~0`, which is trivially satisfied by any non-degenerate `impact_code` scoring — the harness cannot detect an `impact_code` regression (e.g. misconfigured tier values) as long as any non-zero separation survives.
findings.location: Section D — A/B harness, AC line: 'EXITS NON-ZERO unless the new model's HIGH↔NIT median separation strictly beats the baseline's'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the `sep_new > sep_baseline` gate with an absolute floor: `sep_new >= MIN_SEPARATION` (e.g. 0.40, matching the ADR-0035 calibration target of `median(HIGH) − median(NIT) > 0.30`) AND `median_NIT < 0.30`. Retain the baseline computation as a reporting metric (not the gate predicate), so the script still shows "mean impact achieves separation X" for documentation, but the CI exit-nonzero condition is the absolute separation floor that cannot be trivially satisfied by a degenerate model.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a unit-test case for `ab_impact_model.py` that feeds a synthetic labeled fixture where the new model's separation does NOT beat the baseline (e.g. swap labels or use a constant-output model), and asserts the script exits non-zero — so the rejection path of the gate is explicitly covered.
findings.finding: The A/B gate script (`ab_impact_model.py`) is tested only for the passing case (new model beats baseline); the critical enforcement path — exit non-zero when the new model does NOT beat the baseline — has no corresponding unit test. This means the gate's rejection logic could be silently broken (e.g. inverted comparison) while the test suite remains green.
findings.suggested_fix: In `tests/unit/test_impact_model_versioning.py`, add a parametrized test case for the A/B gate: construct a `records` list where `impact_code` produces no separation (all outputs equal or reversed), call the gate function, and assert it raises `SystemExit` with a non-zero code (or returns `False` / raises, per implementation). This mirrors the existing positive case and fully covers the binary gate.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in Section D what the baseline `impact()` computation means on the `code_review_impact_labels.jsonl` fixture — which has no old-model attrs — by either (a) specifying that the fixture should be annotated with old-model attrs for the baseline comparison, (b) replacing the baseline with a trivial constant (e.g. uniform 0.25) and documenting that the gate only verifies the new model achieves a minimum absolute separation, or (c) naming a different, old-attr-carrying reference set as the baseline corpus.
findings.finding: The A/B harness in Section D specifies 'the baseline (the kernel mean `impact`)' scored against `code_review_impact_labels.jsonl`, but the fixture exclusively carries new-model binary attrs (consequence binaries, trigger_likelihood, churn90, etc.) with NO old-model attrs (prod_impact, debt_impact, blast_radius, likelihood, reversibility). Calling the kernel `impact()` on every fixture row will return a near-constant ~0.25 (all ordinal attrs at their defaults), producing near-zero baseline separation. The gate condition `sep_new > sep_baseline` then trivially passes — not because the new model is better, but because the baseline comparison is degenerate. The plan never specifies what the baseline is actually computing on this fixture.
findings.location: Section D — `docs/experiments/ab_impact_model.py` specification; AC line: '`docs/experiments/ab_impact_model.py` scores `code_review_impact_labels.jsonl` with the new model vs the baseline mean'
findings.suggested_fix: In Section D, replace 'a BASELINE (the kernel mean `impact`)' with a specified minimum absolute separation threshold (e.g. 'sep_new > 0.30') against the fixture, since the fixture carries no old-model attrs and the kernel mean `impact()` is not meaningfully comparable on new-schema data. Document this substitution in the ADR (Section E) as the co-calibration target ADR-0035 names: `median(impact_code[HIGH]) − median(impact_code[NIT]) > 0.30`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `test_impact_code_gap_beats_old_mean_gap` in `tests/unit/test_impact_code.py` as the existing pinned equivalent of the Section D A/B gate, and clarify whether `ab_impact_model.py` should import/delegate to it or replace it — to avoid two independently-maintained gate definitions.
findings.finding: The A/B gate logic in Section D (`sep_new > sep_baseline`, scored against `code_review_impact_labels.jsonl`) is already implemented and pinned as a production regression test `test_impact_code_gap_beats_old_mean_gap` in `tests/unit/test_impact_code.py`. The plan does not enumerate this existing test as an overlap, so `ab_impact_model.py` will duplicate the same comparison without acknowledging it, leaving two divergent gate definitions that could drift.
findings.suggested_fix: In Section D, add a note: 'NOTE: `test_impact_code_gap_beats_old_mean_gap` in `test_impact_code.py` already pins this invariant as a unit test. `ab_impact_model.py` wraps it as a runnable CLI gate for manual/CI invocation; the script should call the same `_median_gap` helper (importable from the test module or extracted to a shared util) rather than re-implementing the computation.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge that `test_secrets_security_keys_are_the_ws5_blocking_handoff` in `test_code_review_ws2.py` already locks the code-review `blocking_enabled` invariant, and clarify whether `test_impact_model_versioning.py` should import/reuse that assertion or simply cross-reference it rather than duplicating it.
findings.finding: The permissive-rollout invariant for CODE-REVIEW (AC line 3: `blocking_enabled: true` set == `{secret-detection, high-critical-security}`) is already locked by the existing test `test_secrets_security_keys_are_the_ws5_blocking_handoff` in `tests/unit/test_code_review_ws2.py`. The plan scopes a NEW test file `tests/unit/test_impact_model_versioning.py` to re-assert this same invariant without enumerating the pre-existing test as an overlap.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact import path and call signature for the baseline 'kernel mean `impact`' computation in Section D (e.g., `from rebar.llm.code_review.impact import impact; baseline = mean([impact(attrs) for attrs in samples])`) so an executor can implement `ab_impact_model.py` without consulting external code.
findings.finding: The A/B baseline is described only as 'the kernel mean `impact`' with no pointer to a specific importable function, attribute, or computation recipe, leaving an executor unable to implement it without external knowledge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence: 'BASELINE is computed by calling the pre-redesign scalar `impact(attrs_dict)` from `src/rebar/llm/code_review/impact_legacy.py` (or whichever module holds the old kernel mean) and taking its median over the labeled set.'  If the legacy function no longer exists as a standalone callable, state the fallback formula explicitly (e.g., arithmetic mean of the raw numeric attrs values).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving command for AC5, e.g., `test -f docs/adr/0036-impact-model-rollout-and-calibration-cadence.md && echo OK`, so file creation at the exact expected path is deterministically verifiable in-session.
findings.finding: AC5 ('A recalibration-cadence ADR is recorded under `docs/adr/`') has no proving command, leaving no deterministic in-session verification that the file was actually created with the expected path.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC5: 'Proving command: `test -f docs/adr/0036-impact-model-rollout-and-calibration-cadence.md && echo OK`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an integration-level or emit-level unit test (e.g., mock the file-write and assert the serialised JSON contains `impact_model_version`) to prove the tag survives the full `build_payload → emit → write` path, not just the payload construction step.
findings.finding: The claim 'emit forwards it [impact_model_version] automatically' is asserted without a proving command that exercises the actual sidecar write path in a real (or realistic integration-level) review run, so the unit tests on `build_payload` alone do not prove the tag reaches the persisted `.json` file.
findings.suggested_fix: In `test_impact_model_versioning.py`, add a test that calls the sidecar's `emit(...)` (with the file-write patched via `tmp_path` or `unittest.mock`) and asserts `json.loads(written_content)['impact_model_version'] == 'plan-v2'` (and `'code-v2'` for the code sidecar).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a 'Version naming and bump rules' subsection to the ADR (E) specifying: the `{model}-v{N}` naming convention, the trigger condition for a bump (e.g., any change to score computation, formula, or weight), and a pointer to the canonical version constants in the sidecar modules.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new versioning scheme (`plan-v2`, `code-v2`, etc.) as a corpus-segmentation contract, but neither section E's ADR nor any other plan section specifies (a) the naming convention for future versions, (b) the trigger rule for when a version bump is required vs. optional, or (c) how a reviewer discovers the current canonical version values. Without this, future maintainers may inconsistently bump versions (or forget to), silently mixing cohorts and defeating the segmentation the whole plan is built on.
findings.location: Section B (versioning), Section E (ADR), Scope / files — docs/adr/0036-impact-model-rollout-and-calibration-cadence.md
findings.suggested_fix:
In docs/adr/0036-impact-model-rollout-and-calibration-cadence.md, add a section such as: '## Impact model version naming
- Canonical values live in `IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION` in each sidecar module.
- Format: `{model}-v{N}` (e.g., `plan-v2`, `code-v2`).
- A bump is required whenever any change to the scoring formula, weights, or criteria set would alter the numeric score of an existing input. Non-scoring changes (logging, formatting) do not require a bump.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback specification for the sidecar `impact_model_version` key (e.g. 'revert commit X, no sidecar cleanup required because the calibrator's missing-tag discipline already handles untagged entries') or gate the new key behind a feature flag so it can be disabled without a code deploy.
findings.finding: The sidecar `impact_model_version` key addition is a one-shot runtime behavior change (every REVIEW_RESULT event in production immediately starts carrying the new key) with no explicit, documented rollback path and no feature-flag or canary stage described.
findings.location: Section B (Impact-model versioning) and Scope / files — `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py`, `src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py`
findings.suggested_fix: In Section B or the cadence ADR, add a one-sentence rollback note: 'To roll back: revert the sidecar module constants commit; previously-emitted tagged sidecars are harmless — the calibrator skips untagged entries by default, so no corpus cleanup is required.' This satisfies the T12 rollback criterion at minimal cost given the additive, read-safe nature of the change.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an empty-corpus guard to `calibrate_plan_review_thresholds.py`: when `--impact-model-version` is set and `load()` returns zero sidecars after filtering, emit a clear warning (e.g., `WARNING: 0 sidecars matched version '<v>'; corpus is empty`) and exit non-zero (or at minimum prominently surface the count), so the operator knows no data was processed rather than receiving vacuous output.
findings.finding: When `--impact-model-version` is supplied to `calibrate_plan_review_thresholds.py` and every sidecar in the corpus is either a different version or has no tag, `load()` silently returns an empty dataset. The plan specifies the skip logic but does not specify any warning, early-exit, or error output for an empty post-filter corpus, so a user who mistyped the version string would receive meaningless (or vacuously 'clean') calibration output with no indication that no data was processed.
findings.location: Section C — `calibrate_plan_review_thresholds.py` `load()` filter logic; AC line: '`load()` SKIPS any sidecar whose `data.get("impact_model_version")` != `<v>`, and a sidecar with NO tag is treated as "unknown" and skipped'
findings.suggested_fix: After the filter loop in `load()`, add: `if not sidecars: sys.exit(f'ERROR: no sidecars matched --impact-model-version {args.impact_model_version}; check version string and corpus path.')` and add a unit test case for this scenario alongside the existing synthetic sidecar set test.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `ab_impact_model.py` (Section D) provides value beyond what `test_impact_code_gap_beats_old_mean_gap` already enforces; if not, replace it with a note pointing to the pinned test, or scope it to add genuinely new functionality the test cannot (e.g. reporting per-row scores, consuming a *new* fixture not already in pytest).
findings.finding: Section D's ab_impact_model.py reconstitutes the identical computation already pinned as a production regression test: `_median_gap(impact_code) > _median_gap(impact)` on `code_review_impact_labels.jsonl` (test_impact_code.py lines 259-281, `test_impact_code_gap_beats_old_mean_gap`). The proposed script adds only a CLI exit-nonzero wrapper around logic that `pytest` already enforces — this is a duplicate of existing codebase functionality (NIH/rebuild), and the 'CI/script-usable gate' value proposition is already delivered by the pinned test failing the build.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) drop `ab_impact_model.py` and document in the ADR that the existing `test_impact_code_gap_beats_old_mean_gap` test IS the gate (it already exits non-zero on CI), or (b) scope the script to a distinct, non-test-duplicating purpose — e.g. consuming version-segmented live sidecars from Section C rather than the static fixture, which the pytest test cannot do.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a rationale paragraph in Section B (and the ADR in E) explaining why bumping the existing `schema` string (the established versioning axis already guarded by `latest_review_result`) is NOT sufficient for formula-version corpus segmentation, before committing to the novel parallel `impact_model_version` key.
findings.finding: Section B introduces a new `impact_model_version` top-level sidecar key as a second, parallel versioning dimension alongside the already-established `schema` key (`plan_review_result_v1` / `code_review_result_v1`), but provides no justification for why the established approach — bumping the schema string, which `latest_review_result` already guards on — is insufficient for corpus segmentation. The plan does not consider bumping `schema` to `'*_v2'` before committing to a novel dual-versioning architecture.
findings.location: Section B ('Impact-model versioning') / both sidecar.py files / AC line 'Both REVIEW_RESULT sidecars stamp a top-level impact_model_version'
findings.suggested_fix: The natural justification is that `schema` controls structural/shape compatibility (readers skip payloads that can't be deserialized safely) while `impact_model_version` controls formula-version calibration segmentation (the calibrator needs to segment even same-schema payloads produced by different formulas). State this explicitly in the ADR so the two axes have distinct, non-overlapping purposes and future maintainers bump the right one.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before implementing `ab_impact_model.py` as a full standalone script, justify in Section D why the existing `test_impact_code_gap_beats_old_mean_gap` test plus `_median_gap` helper (tests/unit/test_impact_code.py:259-281) is insufficient, and what the script adds that could not be a thin wrapper or `--report` flag on the existing test/helper.
findings.finding: Section D's proposed `docs/experiments/ab_impact_model.py` reconstructs the same gate computation already pinned as a production regression test (`tests/unit/test_impact_code.py:259-281`, `_median_gap` helper, `test_impact_code_gap_beats_old_mean_gap`) against the identical fixture (`code_review_impact_labels.jsonl`). The plan does not consider whether the existing test — or a thin `--report` flag on the current test invocation — already satisfies the stated A/B goal, before committing to a full standalone script.
findings.location: Section D ('Diff-grounded A/B harness') / docs/experiments/ab_impact_model.py (proposed new) vs tests/unit/test_impact_code.py:259-281
findings.suggested_fix: If the key addition is a human-readable CLI report and a portable exit-code outside pytest, document that explicitly; then implement `ab_impact_model.py` as a thin wrapper that imports and calls the existing `_median_gap` helper from the test module (or promote `_median_gap` to a shared utility) rather than re-implementing the computation, keeping a single source of truth.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
